name: Inflammation Resolution Theory of Post-Conflict Reconciliation
executive_summary:
Immunology has undergone a paradigm shift in the past two decades with the discovery that inflammation resolution is not the passive decay of pro-inflammatory signals, but an actively orchestrated process requiring the biosynthesis of specialized pro-resolving mediators (SPMs) — lipoxins, resolvins, protectins, and maresins. Without active resolution, inflammation becomes chronic: the tissue remains infiltrated by immune cells, damage continues, and the system enters a self-sustaining cycle of injury and inflammatory response. This framework maps the inflammation-resolution cascade onto post-conflict societies, arguing that reconciliation after war or mass violence follows the same active-switch logic. The initial pro-inflammatory phase — truth commissions, war crimes tribunals, public acknowledgment of atrocities, media exposure of perpetrator identities — is the societal immune response: necessary, adaptive, and oriented toward identifying and neutralizing the source of harm. But this phase cannot resolve itself through exhaustion alone.

The critical insight is that the switch from pro-inflammatory to pro-resolving phase requires active biosynthesis of resolution mediators — in societal terms, the deliberate creation of restorative justice practitioners, cultural bridge-builders, shared economic institutions, and symbolic reconciliation acts (joint memorials, integrated schools, cross-community employment programs). These mediators do not suppress the inflammatory response (that would be immunosuppression, analogous to forced amnesia or censorship); instead, they actively promote tissue repair by reprogramming macrophages from a destructive M1 phenotype to a reparative M2 phenotype. In societal terms, this means transforming the public narrative from one of identification-and-punishment to one of reconstruction-and-coexistence, without denying the validity of the initial inflammatory response.

Societies that fail to produce resolution mediators in adequate quantity or at the right time enter chronic sociopolitical inflammation — perpetual grievance cycles, generational trauma transmission, periodic flare-ups of sectarian violence, and institutional paralysis born of unresolved enmity. The framework predicts that the timing of the switch is critical: initiating resolution mechanisms too early (before the pro-inflammatory phase has adequately identified and contained the threat) produces suppressive pseudo-resolution analogous to immunosuppressive drugs that mask but do not resolve infection. Initiating them too late allows chronic inflammation to establish self-reinforcing feedback loops (polarized media ecosystems, grievance-based political parties, segregated institutions) that resist resolution even when mediators are eventually introduced. The therapeutic window for introducing resolution mediators is narrow and context-dependent, but the framework provides a structured way to diagnose where a post-conflict society sits in the cascade and what interventions are appropriate at each stage.

mapped_components.unit: Individual reconciliation processes within post-conflict communities, each involving specific perpetrator-victim relationships, community narratives, institutional reforms, and mediator interventions that collectively determine whether the society resolves or enters chronic inflammation
mapped_components.variation: Differences in resolution trajectory arising from the type and intensity of the original conflict, the adequacy of the pro-inflammatory phase (completeness of truth-telling, credibility of tribunals), the availability and cultural legitimacy of resolution mediators, and the timing of the inflammatory-to-resolving switch
mapped_components.selection: Societal survival pressure: post-conflict societies that achieve successful resolution rebuild functional governance, economic productivity, and social cohesion; those trapped in chronic inflammation experience ongoing instability, brain drain, economic stagnation, and vulnerability to conflict recurrence
mapped_components.fitness: The completeness and durability of reconciliation, measured by inter-group trust indices, cross-community institutional participation, reduction in revenge violence, economic integration across former conflict lines, and the absence of conflict recurrence within two generations
mapped_components.boundary: The post-conflict society as a bounded system with finite capacity for both inflammatory and resolution responses; external actors (international organizations, diaspora communities, neighboring states) function as exogenous immune signals that can either amplify inflammation or supply resolution mediators

falsifiable_predictions: Post-conflict societies that establish formal restorative justice programs (resolution mediators) between 3 and 10 years after conflict cessation will show significantly higher inter-group trust scores at the 25-year mark than societies that introduce them before 3 years (premature resolution) or after 15 years (chronic inflammation establishment), producing a detectable inverted-U relationship between mediator timing and reconciliation outcomes

The introduction of resolution mechanisms without a preceding adequate pro-inflammatory phase (truth-telling, accountability) will produce worse long-term reconciliation outcomes than no formal intervention at all, analogous to immunosuppression masking an unresolved infection — measurable as higher rates of conflict recurrence within 30 years

Societies in chronic sociopolitical inflammation will show measurable biomarker-analogous signatures: sustained elevated media negativity about the former adversary, low rates of cross-community voluntary association membership, high rates of in-group residential clustering beyond what economic factors predict, and elevated cortisol levels in populations living near former conflict zones

The effectiveness of resolution mediators (restorative justice practitioners, cultural bridge-builders) will depend on their perceived neutrality and community legitimacy, analogous to how SPM effectiveness depends on receptor compatibility — mediators perceived as aligned with one side will fail to trigger the resolution switch even if structurally identical to effective mediators

Cross-community economic institutions (joint businesses, shared markets, integrated workplaces) will function as the most potent resolution mediators, analogous to resolvins being the most potent SPM class, because they create material interdependence that actively reprograms intergroup attitudes from adversarial to cooperative

Post-conflict societies with diaspora populations that maintain pro-inflammatory narratives (grievance preservation, conflict fundraising) will show delayed or incomplete resolution, analogous to systemic inflammatory signals from distant tissue sites that sustain local inflammation even after local resolution mediators are introduced

supporting_evidence.finding: The discovery that inflammation resolution requires active biosynthesis of specialized pro-resolving mediators (resolvins, protectins, lipoxins) rather than passive decay of pro-inflammatory signals fundamentally changed immunological understanding of chronic inflammatory disease — resolution failure, not excessive inflammation initiation, drives chronicity
supporting_evidence.source: Serhan, C.N. (2014). Pro-resolving lipid mediators are leads for resolution physiology. Nature, 510(7503), 92-101
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the core biological mechanism: resolution is an active process requiring specific mediator biosynthesis, directly informing the framework's central claim that post-conflict reconciliation requires deliberate production of resolution mechanisms rather than passive time-healing

supporting_evidence.finding: Comparative analysis of post-conflict societies shows that truth commissions followed by restorative justice programs produce more durable peace than truth commissions alone or amnesty without truth-telling, but only when the restorative phase is introduced after the truth commission has completed its accountability function
supporting_evidence.source: Mendeloff, D. (2004). Truth-seeking, truth-telling, and postconflict peacebuilding: Curb the enthusiasm? International Studies Review, 6(3), 355-380; supplemented by Theidon, K. (2006). Justice in transition. Journal of Conflict Resolution, 50(3), 433-457
supporting_evidence.relevance: Supports the sequential phasing prediction: the pro-inflammatory phase (truth-telling) must precede the resolution phase (restorative justice) for durable outcomes, and premature resolution undermines long-term reconciliation

supporting_evidence.finding: South Africa's Truth and Reconciliation Commission, often cited as a successful inflammation-to-resolution transition, showed measurable improvements in inter-racial trust and reduction in political violence in the decade following its conclusion, but communities where the TRC process was perceived as incomplete (inadequate pro-inflammatory phase) showed persistent grievance and lower reconciliation scores
supporting_evidence.source: Gibson, J.L. (2004). Overcoming Apartheid: Can Truth Reconcile a Divided Nation? Russell Sage Foundation
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides a natural experiment supporting the framework's prediction that resolution mediator effectiveness depends on the completeness of the preceding pro-inflammatory phase — communities with inadequate truth-telling could not achieve resolution despite identical mediator interventions

supporting_evidence.finding: Research on Northern Ireland's peace process documents how cross-community economic programs (shared workplaces, joint tourism initiatives) were more effective at reducing sectarian attitudes than symbolic reconciliation gestures or formal political agreements alone, with the effect mediated by personal contact and material interdependence
supporting_evidence.source: Hewstone, M. et al. (2006). Intergroup contact, forgiveness, and experience of 'The Troubles' in Northern Ireland. Journal of Social Issues, 62(1), 99-120
supporting_evidence.relevance: Supports the prediction that economic integration functions as the most potent resolution mediator class, analogous to the high potency of resolvins in biological inflammation resolution, because it creates structural interdependence that actively reprograms intergroup attitudes

experimental_designs.title: Cross-National Resolution Timing Analysis
experimental_designs.description: Compile a dataset of 40-50 post-conflict societies (post-1945) with documented conflict end dates, truth commission or tribunal start dates, and restorative justice or formal reconciliation program launch dates. Code the delay between conflict end and pro-inflammatory phase, the delay between pro-inflammatory phase and resolution mechanism introduction, and the completeness of each phase using standardized rubrics. Measure reconciliation outcomes at 10, 20, and 30 years using the Inter-Group Trust Index, Minorities at Risk dataset, and UCDP conflict recurrence data. Test the inverted-U prediction: reconciliation outcomes should peak when resolution mechanisms are introduced within a specific window (predicted: 3-10 years after adequate pro-inflammatory phase completion), with worse outcomes for both premature and delayed introduction. Control for conflict intensity, external intervention, economic development, and regime type.

experimental_designs.title: Chronic Inflammation Biomarker Validation
experimental_designs.description: In three societies identified as being in chronic sociopolitical inflammation (sustained post-conflict grievance cycles persisting more than 25 years without resolution), measure a battery of inflammation-analogous indicators: media sentiment analysis of cross-community reporting (pro-inflammatory cytokine proxy), residential segregation indices controlling for economic factors (tissue compartmentalization proxy), cross-community voluntary association membership rates (immune cell phenotype proxy — M1 versus M2), and population cortisol levels in communities near former conflict zones (systemic stress response proxy). Compare these indicators against matched post-conflict societies that have achieved resolution and against non-post-conflict control societies. The framework predicts that chronic-inflammation societies will show a distinctive signature on all four indicators that distinguishes them from both resolved and non-conflict societies.

experimental_designs.title: Diaspora Inflammation Amplification Study
experimental_designs.description: Identify 10 post-conflict societies with significant diaspora populations and code the diaspora's orientation as pro-inflammatory (maintaining grievance narratives, funding conflict commemoration, lobbying for punitive action) or pro-resolution (supporting reconciliation initiatives, funding cross-community programs, facilitating economic integration). Compare reconciliation trajectories of homeland societies with pro-inflammatory versus pro-resolution diasporas, controlling for conflict characteristics and domestic factors. The framework predicts that pro-inflammatory diasporas measurably delay or prevent the resolution switch, analogous to systemic inflammatory signals from distant tissue sites. This could be tested using paired cases: Sri Lankan Tamil diaspora communities with differing political orientations and their differential influence on homeland reconciliation processes.

limitations: The inflammation resolution cascade is a molecular process operating on timescales of hours to days, while post-conflict reconciliation unfolds over years to decades — the temporal mapping is metaphorical rather than mechanistic, and the framework does not specify which societal processes correspond to the specific enzymatic pathways (COX-2, 5-LOX, 15-LOX) that produce biological SPMs

The framework may oversimplify the plurality of post-conflict justice approaches by shoehorning them into a binary inflammatory/resolving classification — retributive justice, transitional justice, traditional justice, and lustration each involve distinct mechanisms that the two-phase model does not differentiate

The 'therapeutic window' prediction is clinically appealing but difficult to test rigorously because each post-conflict situation has unique contextual factors, and the window boundaries (3-10 years) are proposed based on pattern observation rather than derived from first principles

The biological model assumes a single organism with unified immune regulation, while post-conflict societies contain multiple competing factions, each with their own inflammatory and resolution dynamics — there is no societal equivalent of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis coordinating the overall response

External geopolitical pressures (sanctions, aid conditionality, regional security dynamics) may dominate reconciliation trajectories in ways that overwhelm the endogenous inflammation-resolution dynamics the framework emphasizes, limiting its explanatory power in cases where great-power politics shapes post-conflict outcomes
potential_applications: Post-conflict intervention sequencing tools for international organizations (UN Peacebuilding Commission, World Bank Fragility Unit) that diagnose where a society sits in the inflammation-resolution cascade and recommend phase-appropriate interventions — resisting pressure to introduce resolution mechanisms before the pro-inflammatory phase has adequately run its course

Early warning systems for chronic sociopolitical inflammation that monitor the media sentiment, residential segregation, and cross-community association indicators identified as biomarker analogues, triggering intervention when a society shows signs of failing to switch to the resolution phase within the predicted window

Diaspora engagement strategies that channel diaspora resources toward resolution mediator production (funding cross-community economic programs, supporting cultural bridge-builder training) rather than pro-inflammatory amplification (grievance maintenance, punitive lobbying), informed by the prediction that diaspora orientation significantly affects homeland resolution trajectory

Training curricula for restorative justice practitioners that explicitly frame their role as resolution mediator biosynthesis — emphasizing the importance of timing, perceived neutrality, and the distinction between active resolution (which acknowledges the inflammatory phase) and suppressive pseudo-resolution (which bypasses it)

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Provides the biological source model — the specific mechanism of active resolution through specialized pro-resolving mediator biosynthesis, the distinction between resolution and suppression, and the pathology of chronic inflammation when the resolution switch fails

Offers the political economy lens for understanding why chronic sociopolitical inflammation persists: conflict-associated capital accumulation (war profiteering, grievance-based political careers, diaspora fundraising) creates economic interests in sustained inflammation that actively resist the resolution switch, analogous to how certain chronic inflammatory conditions involve cells that benefit metabolically from ongoing inflammation

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Liminality as Psychosocial Phase Transition (Deep Dive 50)
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Both frameworks describe the critical importance of phase transitions in human systems: the liminality framework describes individual identity transitions between stable states, while the inflammation-resolution framework describes collective transitions from conflict to peace — both require active facilitation of the transition rather than passive waiting

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Stimulated Emission Theory of Rhetoric (Deep Dive 49)
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: The rhetorical stimulated emission framework explains a mechanism by which pro-inflammatory narratives self-amplify (coherent messaging that recruits aligned audiences into synchronized grievance expression), while the inflammation-resolution framework identifies the conditions under which this amplification can be redirected toward resolution messaging through the introduction of appropriately timed mediator signals
